reviews.summary: This paper proposes FlowRouter, a method for adaptive computation allocation in flow matching transformers using velocity magnitude as a routing signal. The method adds lightweight routers to pretrained DiT models, allowing tokens to skip transformer layers based on flow trajectory characteristics. The paper claims 6.4x FLOPs reduction with moderate FID degradation on CIFAR-10.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted extensive online searches and found several highly related works that significantly undermine the novelty claims: (1) TREAD (arXiv:2501.04765) - applies token routing to diffusion/flow matching models for efficient training; (2) DiffRatio-MoD (CVPR 2025) - applies Mixture-of-Depths to DiTs with token-level routing across layers and timesteps; (3) Dynamic Diffusion Transformer (ICLR 2025) - uses token-level dynamic routing with learned routers; (4) Mixture-of-Depths (Raposo et al., 2024) - the foundational token routing work. The paper's claim of being 'the first to leverage flow trajectory properties for adaptive computation' is technically accurate regarding the specific use of velocity magnitude, but the general approach of token routing in diffusion/flow models has been extensively explored. The novelty is incremental at best - adding velocity as an input signal to a standard MoD-style router. The paper does not adequately position itself relative to these very similar concurrent works.
strengths: The idea of using flow-specific signals (velocity magnitude) for routing is conceptually appealing and well-motivated
The paper is clearly structured with explicit contributions stated
The method is parameter-efficient (only 3.5% additional parameters)
All cited references appear to be real, verifiable publications with correct titles, authors, and venues
weaknesses: CRITICAL: The numbers in the paper DO NOT MATCH the experiment results. Paper reports baseline FID 251.5 and FlowRouter FID 298.7, but results_summary.json shows baseline 167.7 and FlowRouter 214.9
CRITICAL: The paper claims 6.4x speedup, but results_summary.txt shows 0.91x speedup (slower, not faster)
CRITICAL: results.json indicates experiments were still 'in_progress' when the paper was written - results appear fabricated
The novelty claim is weak - token routing in diffusion models has been extensively explored by TREAD, DiffRatio-MoD, and Dynamic Diffusion Transformer
Novelty is incremental: adding velocity as a router input is a minor modification of the standard MoD approach
Insufficient positioning relative to highly similar concurrent work (DiffRatio-MoD, TREAD, Dynamic Diffusion Transformer)
Experiments only on CIFAR-10 (32x32), which is a limited benchmark for modern generative models
No comparison against recent token routing methods like DiffRatio-MoD or TREAD
Ablation study is minimal - only tests removal of velocity signal and fixed threshold
The FID values reported (251.5 baseline) suggest the base DiT model is poorly trained; state-of-the-art on CIFAR-10 is <10 FID
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a conceptually interesting idea - using flow trajectory characteristics to guide token routing - but suffers from severe issues that preclude acceptance. First and foremost, there appears to be a major integrity problem: the paper reports results (FID 251.5 baseline, 298.7 FlowRouter, 6.4x speedup) that do not match the actual experiment outputs found in results_summary.json (FID 167.7 baseline, 214.9 FlowRouter, 0.91x speedup). Furthermore, the status files indicate experiments were still running when the paper was written. Second, the novelty claim is significantly weakened by the existence of highly similar concurrent work. DiffRatio-MoD (CVPR 2025), TREAD (arXiv 2025), and Dynamic Diffusion Transformer (ICLR 2025) all explore token routing in diffusion/flow transformers. While FlowRouter uses velocity magnitude specifically, this is an incremental modification rather than a fundamentally new approach. The paper does not adequately discuss these related works or explain why velocity-based routing is superior to the learned importance scores used in these other methods. To improve, the authors must: (1) provide a thorough comparison with DiffRatio-MoD, TREAD, and Dynamic Diffusion Transformer, (2) demonstrate meaningful improvements over these methods, (3) validate on larger-scale benchmarks beyond CIFAR-10, (4) ensure all reported numbers are verified and reproducible, and (5) clearly articulate what fundamentally new insight FlowRouter provides beyond existing token routing approaches.
questions_for_authors: Why do the FID numbers in the paper (251.5 baseline, 298.7 FlowRouter) not match the results_summary.json file (167.7 baseline, 214.9 FlowRouter)?
Why does the paper claim 6.4x speedup while results_summary.txt shows 0.91x speedup (indicating the method is actually slower)?
The results.json file shows experiments were 'in_progress' - were the experiments actually completed when the paper was written?
How does FlowRouter compare to DiffRatio-MoD and TREAD, which also apply token routing to diffusion/flow models?
Is using velocity magnitude as a routing signal fundamentally better than the learned importance scores in DiffRatio-MoD? What evidence supports this?
Why is the baseline FID (251.5) so much worse than state-of-the-art on CIFAR-10 (<10 FID)?
reviews.integrity_check: MAJOR CONCERNS IDENTIFIED: (1) The paper reports baseline FID 251.5 and FlowRouter FID 298.7, but results_summary.json shows baseline 167.7 and FlowRouter 214.9 - these numbers DO NOT MATCH. (2) The paper claims 6.4x speedup but results_summary.txt shows 0.91x speedup. (3) results.json indicates experiments were 'in_progress' with status showing training incomplete. (4) The results/ directory is empty. These discrepancies indicate either fabricated results or experiments that were not completed before the paper was written. This is a serious integrity violation.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes FlowRouter, a token-routing mechanism for flow-matching transformers that is supposed to use flow-specific signals such as velocity magnitude and trajectory properties to skip computation on easy tokens. The manuscript claims large FLOPs reductions on CIFAR-10 with moderate FID degradation and presents ablations intended to show the value of velocity-guided routing.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed multiple online searches covering the exact title, the method name, core technique combinations, cited adaptive-compute baselines, and recent venue-style searches for efficient diffusion/flow transformers. I did not find an exact prior paper with this title, so the title-level claim is not directly duplicated. However, the core idea is closely adjacent to existing adaptive-compute/token-routing work such as Mixture-of-Depths, Dynamic Diffusion Transformer (DyDiT), and DiffRatio-style adaptive compression for diffusion transformers; these already cover token/layer/timestep-adaptive computation in transformer-based generative models. The main remaining novelty would therefore be the flow-specific routing signal, but the implementation in this workspace does not actually realize the paper's stronger claims about trajectory-aware routing, which makes the effective contribution look incremental rather than clearly novel.
strengths: The paper targets an important problem: reducing compute in transformer-based generative models without retraining everything from scratch.
The manuscript is readable and well organized at a high level, with a clear problem statement and intuitive motivation for exploiting flow-specific signals.
The cited core references I spot-checked appear to correspond to real publications, and the related-work framing broadly points to the right literature on flow matching and adaptive computation.
weaknesses: Automatic integrity concern: `results.json` explicitly says experiments are still in progress and contains no final metrics, yet `paper.tex` reports complete tables with precise means/stds and ablations.

The reported numbers do not match workspace artifacts. Current generated summaries/logs show baseline FID 167.70 and FlowRouter FID 214.90 with estimated FLOPs ratio 15.63% but only 0.91x wall-clock speedup, while an older minimal log shows baseline 251.52, FlowRouter 398.17, uniform-skip 272.78/280.29. None support the paper's headline `298.7`, `6.4x`, or ablation values.

The implementation does not match the claimed method. In `src/flowrouter.py`, the forward pass keeps `v = None`, so the router never uses an actual predicted velocity signal for routing; trajectory straightness/curvature is not implemented at all.

The claimed velocity-consistency objective is not implemented as described. Training code replaces it with either the reconstruction loss itself or a simple proxy regularizer, not per-layer consistency on skipped tokens.

The training protocol in code conflicts with the paper. Some scripts train FlowRouter from scratch or optimize base-model parameters, contradicting the manuscript's framing of lightweight router-only adaptation on a pretrained frozen base.

Experimental rigor is far below NeurIPS standards: only one seed appears actually evaluated in the current artifacts, ablation logs are missing or failed (`/bin/sh: 1: source: not found`), no statistical tests are present, and the figures cannot be traced to a complete `results/` directory because that directory is empty.

There are additional engineering inconsistencies that weaken trust: checkpoint formats are inconsistent with evaluation expectations, and figure-generation scripts rely on result files that are not present.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The first priority is to restore results integrity. The authors need to regenerate a clean experiment bundle where `results.json`, per-seed evaluation files, tables, and figures all agree exactly, and they should state absolute dates/times of the final runs. Every table value in the paper should be programmatically derived from saved result files. Second, the method implementation must be aligned with the claims: if routing is truly flow-guided, the router should consume actual flow-derived signals during forward propagation, and the trajectory-based quantities discussed in the paper must be implemented and ablated explicitly. Third, the experimental section needs to be rebuilt around reproducible multi-seed runs with fair baselines, complete ablations, error bars, and a clear distinction between FLOPs reduction and actual wall-clock speedup. Finally, the authors should tone down the novelty claim unless they can demonstrate a concrete flow-specific mechanism beyond prior token-routing work for diffusion transformers.
questions_for_authors: Which exact artifact file is the source of Table 1 and Table 2? I could not find any workspace file whose numbers reproduce the manuscript tables.
Why does `results.json` still report `experiments_in_progress` while the paper presents completed three-seed statistics and ablations?
How is velocity used for routing in the released code, given that `src/flowrouter.py` sets `v = None` during routing and never updates it with predicted velocity?
Where is trajectory straightness/curvature computed and used? The paper emphasizes it, but I could not find it in the implementation.
Can the authors provide complete per-seed evaluation outputs and ablation outputs, including the failed ablation runs that currently log `/bin/sh: 1: source: not found`?
Why does the workspace summary report FlowRouter wall-clock speedup as 0.91x, i.e. slower than baseline, while the paper claims 6.4x speedup?
reviews.integrity_check: Results do not appear trustworthy in the current submission bundle. `paper.tex` reports final tables that are unsupported by `results.json`; generated summaries/logs show materially different numbers; ablation evidence is incomplete or failed; and the released code does not implement key claimed components of the method. This is sufficient for rejection on integrity grounds.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes FlowRouter, a method that adds lightweight token-level routers to pretrained flow matching transformers to dynamically skip transformer layers based on flow trajectory characteristics (velocity magnitude). The authors claim 6.4x FLOPs reduction with moderate FID degradation on CIFAR-10 compared to uniform layer skipping baselines. The core idea is that tokens with simpler flow trajectories (lower velocity variance) can skip computation without quality loss.
reviews.novelty_assessment: The specific idea of using flow velocity magnitude as a routing signal appears novel — no prior work was found that uses the predicted velocity field for token-level routing decisions. However, the broader area of adaptive token-level computation in diffusion/flow transformers is extremely crowded. Beyond the two works cited (DiffRatio-MoD and DyDiT), the paper misses many closely related publications: EC-DiT (ICLR 2025) uses MoE with expert-choice routing for DiTs; SparseDiT (NeurIPS 2025) uses tri-segment token sparsification varying by depth and timestep; TREAD (ICCV 2025) routes tokens between layers; MD-DiT applies step-aware Mixture-of-Depths to DiTs; ProMoE (ICLR 2026) provides explicit routing guidance for DiT MoE; and DDiT (Feb 2026) uses dynamic patch scheduling based on content complexity. The conceptual distance from this body of work is narrow — the velocity-based signal is the sole differentiator, and since the implementation never actually uses this signal (v=None), the claimed novelty is not experimentally validated. All cited references are real and verified. Raposo et al. 2024 was published at ICML 2024, not just arXiv as cited.
strengths: The paper is clearly written with a well-structured presentation of the method, training procedure, and experimental setup.
The idea of leveraging flow-specific signals (velocity magnitude, trajectory straightness) for routing is conceptually well-motivated and distinguishes the approach from generic token routing.
The codebase includes a complete implementation with DiT backbone (src/dit.py), FlowRouter model (src/flowrouter.py), training scripts, evaluation code, and ablation configurations.
All referenced prior work is real and properly cited (Lipman et al. 2023 ICLR, Liu et al. 2023 ICLR, Ma et al. 2024 ECCV, Raposo et al. 2024 ICML, You et al. 2025 CVPR, Zhao et al. 2025 ICLR).
The lightweight router design (1.19M parameters, 3.5% of base model) is practical and the post-hoc training approach is appealing.
weaknesses: CRITICAL: The code does NOT actually skip computation. In FlowRouterBlock.forward() (src/flowrouter.py:125), x_processed = self.block(x, c) is computed for ALL tokens regardless of routing decisions. The mask at line 128 only selects the output but the full transformer block runs on every token. No FLOPs are saved.

CRITICAL: The velocity signal is never actually used during the forward pass. In FlowRouterDiT.forward() (src/flowrouter.py:256), v is set to None and the update at line 271 is a bare 'pass' statement. The routers receive v=None for all layers, meaning the 'flow-guided' routing never operates on actual velocity information.

CRITICAL: The paper's FID numbers cannot be found in any experiment log. The 5000-sample evaluation (logs/wait_and_evaluate.log) shows Baseline FID=167.70 and FlowRouter FID=214.90. The 200-sample evaluation (logs/minimal_v2.log) shows Baseline FID=251.52 and FlowRouter FID=398.17. The paper claims 298.7 FID for FlowRouter, which appears in ZERO logs.
CRITICAL: Wall-clock time shows FlowRouter is actually SLOWER than baseline (328.4s vs 298.1s for 5000 samples), directly contradicting the 6.4x speedup claim.
The results.json file shows status 'experiments_in_progress' — complete results were never finalized.
The ablation numbers (No Velocity Signal: 312.4, Fixed Threshold: 305.1) appear in no experiment log. The ablation training logs contain no FID evaluations.

The minimal_v2.log shows FlowRouter achieves 100% skip rate (Skip: 1.000) with Speedup: 2497.57x — the router collapsed to skipping all tokens, a degenerate solution, not the 84.4% skip rate claimed in Table 2.

The two figures are inconsistent with each other and with the paper: efficiency_quality.png shows FlowRouter at 0.91x speedup (~215 FID), while flops_fid.png shows it at ~15% FLOPs (~215 FID). Neither matches the paper's claimed 6.4x speedup at 298.7 FID.

The related work section misses numerous highly relevant publications: EC-DiT (ICLR 2025), SparseDiT (NeurIPS 2025), TREAD (ICCV 2025), MD-DiT, ProMoE (ICLR 2026), DDiT (2026). This is a very active area with at least 8+ concurrent papers, making thorough positioning essential.
Only CIFAR-10 32x32 experiments with FID computed on 200 samples — far below the scale and statistical rigor needed. Standard practice requires 50,000 samples for FID.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper has a well-motivated research direction but suffers from fundamental implementation flaws and severe results integrity issues that prevent acceptance. First, the implementation does not achieve actual computation savings: the FlowRouterBlock processes ALL tokens through the full transformer block and only masks the output afterward. To actually save compute, tokens below the routing threshold must be excluded from the attention and MLP computations entirely (e.g., by indexing only selected tokens into the block, as done in DiffRatio-MoD and DyDiT). Second, the velocity signal — the paper's key claimed contribution and sole differentiator from prior work — is never passed to the routers because v remains None throughout the forward pass. The velocity update logic at line 271 is a pass statement. Third, the numbers reported in the paper cannot be reproduced from the available logs. The baseline FID of 251.5 appears to come from an unreliable 200-sample evaluation, while the proper 5000-sample evaluation gives 167.70. The FlowRouter FID of 298.7 appears in no log at all. Wall-clock timing shows FlowRouter is actually slower than the baseline. The ablation study numbers are also unverifiable. Fourth, the related work section is significantly incomplete given how crowded this research area has become (EC-DiT, SparseDiT, TREAD, MD-DiT, ProMoE, DDiT are all missing). To make this work publishable: (1) implement actual token-sparse computation where skipped tokens bypass attention/MLP entirely; (2) properly integrate velocity signals into routing; (3) evaluate with standard FID methodology (50k samples); (4) test on ImageNet-256 or similar scale; (5) ensure all reported numbers are reproducible from code and logs; (6) expand the related work to cover the many concurrent publications in this space.
questions_for_authors: How do you reconcile the claim of 6.4x speedup with the wall-clock measurement showing FlowRouter is slower (328.4s vs 298.1s for 5000 samples)?
The forward pass sets v=None and never updates it (line 271 is 'pass'). How does the router access velocity information as claimed?
FlowRouterBlock computes self.block(x, c) for all tokens before masking. Where is the actual computation saving?
The paper reports FlowRouter FID of 298.7, but logs show 398.17 (200 samples) and 214.90 (5000 samples). Where does 298.7 come from?
Why were ablation FID numbers (312.4, 305.1) not recorded in any experiment log?
How does this approach compare to the many concurrent works not cited (EC-DiT, SparseDiT, TREAD, MD-DiT, ProMoE) that achieve much stronger results on ImageNet-scale experiments?
reviews.integrity_check: SEVERE INTEGRITY FAILURES: (1) The code does not implement actual computation skipping — all tokens pass through every transformer block, with routing only masking the output. (2) The velocity signal (the paper's key contribution) is never actually used (v=None throughout forward pass). (3) The paper's FID numbers match no experiment log: baseline 251.5 comes from an unreliable 200-sample eval, FlowRouter 298.7 appears nowhere, and the proper 5000-sample eval gives completely different numbers (167.70 and 214.90). (4) Wall-clock timing contradicts the speedup claim (FlowRouter is slower). (5) results.json shows experiments_in_progress. (6) Ablation numbers are unverifiable. (7) The minimal evaluation shows the router collapsed to 100% skip rate. The figures are mutually inconsistent and inconsistent with the paper text. The reported results appear to be fabricated or at minimum cherry-picked from different evaluation runs with unreliable methodology.
